required.title: [Reference] Figure Drawing for All It's Worth
required.author: Andrew Loomis

suggested.title: [Study] Master drawing collections
suggested.author: Rembrandt, Durer, Degas, Michelangelo, Kathe Kollwitz, and Leonardo. Study the originals in museums whenever possible. A drawing seen in person reveals brushwork, pressure, erasure, and revision invisible in reproduction.

supplementary.title: Figure drawing reference photographs or a willing model

competence_markers.description: The student can render a still life with accurate proportion, full value range (highlight through cast shadow), and convincing three-dimensional form
The student can construct a perspective drawing in both one-point and two-point perspective, and can identify perspective effects in observational drawing
The student can complete gesture drawings that capture figure movement and the line of action in 30-second to 2-minute poses
The student can draw a portrait from observation with accurate proportional placement of features and convincing value rendering
The student demonstrates sight-size measurement as a habitual practice when drawing from observation
The student can analyze a master drawing and articulate its compositional, value, and technical decisions
The student works competently in both graphite and charcoal, selecting the appropriate medium for the task
The student maintains a regular sketchbook practice with evidence of sustained, disciplined observation drawing

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain how light creates the illusion of three-dimensional form in a drawing.
narration_prompts.detail: Walk me through the pattern — from highlight to cast shadow. Why does understanding this pattern matter?

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me a still life drawing and explain your process.
narration_prompts.detail: How did you start? Where did you measure? What was the hardest part? What would you do differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between one-point and two-point perspective?
narration_prompts.detail: When do you encounter each in the real world? Draw a quick example of each.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a gesture drawing session.
narration_prompts.detail: What were you trying to capture? What happens when you try to draw too much detail in 30 seconds? What have you learned about seeing movement?

narration_prompts.prompt: Choose a master drawing and analyze it.
narration_prompts.detail: How did the artist use value? Where is the focal point? What decisions did the artist make about what to include and what to leave out?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the most common proportion error in portrait drawing, and why does the brain make it?
narration_prompts.detail: How do you correct for it?

cross_connections.description: Value is the bridge between drawing and painting. Every concept of light and shadow learned here applies directly when color is added. The student who understands value in graphite will understand value in color.
Figure drawing and proportion connect directly to sculptural work. The student who can draw the figure understands the three-dimensional form they will sculpt.
Observational drawing remains a powerful scientific tool. Perspective involves optics and geometry. The anatomy studied for figure drawing connects to biology.
Technical drawing, measured drawing, and the ability to sketch a design before building connect drawing directly to making. The carpenter who can draw a joint wastes less material.
Perspective construction is applied geometry. Proportional measurement is ratio and comparison. The value scale is a study in equal intervals.
Sustained observational drawing is one of the most direct expressions of IC2 in the curriculum. The gap between a careless sketch and a careful study is the gap IC2 exists to close.

content.why_this_matters:
You have learned to look carefully and to draw from observation. Now you are going to develop the specific technical skills that make accurate, expressive drawing possible. This is the tier where drawing becomes a discipline — where you learn the tools that allow you to put on paper what your eye sees, with conviction and precision.

The skills you are about to learn are not modern inventions. They are the same skills taught in every serious art academy for centuries: how to render light and shadow (value), how to measure and compare proportions accurately, how to construct perspective, and how to draw the human figure. These skills were mastered by every artist whose work you admire — from Durer and Rembrandt to Kathe Kollwitz and Andrew Wyeth. None of them were born knowing how to draw. They learned through systematic practice, and so will you.

A crucial principle: the ability to draw what you see is not the endpoint of visual art. It is the foundation from which all other visual work proceeds. The artist who can draw accurately can then choose abstraction, stylization, illustration, design, or any other approach from a position of capability rather than limitation. The artist who skips this foundation has fewer choices, not more.

This packet will be demanding. Representational drawing requires the patience to look, the humility to measure, and the discipline to correct what you see is wrong. But the rewards are extraordinary: you will begin to see the visual world with a depth and precision that most people never develop, and you will be able to put what you see onto paper in a way that communicates to anyone who looks at it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Value rendering** — the ability to see and draw the full range of light to dark, creating the illusion of three-dimensional form
2. **Proportional measurement** — systematic methods for comparing sizes and relationships in a subject
3. **Perspective construction** — understanding and applying one-point and two-point perspective, plus atmospheric perspective
4. **Figure drawing** — gesture drawing, basic anatomical landmarks, and portrait fundamentals
5. **Media competence** — working confidently in graphite and charcoal
6. **Sustained observational practice** — regular, disciplined drawing from life
7. **Visual analysis** — the ability to look at a master drawing and understand how it was constructed
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Value — The Language of Light and Shadow

Value is the range from light to dark. It is the single most important element in representational drawing — more important than line, more important than detail, more important than accuracy of outline. A drawing with correct values looks three-dimensional and real. A drawing with incorrect values looks flat no matter how precise the outline.

**The Value Scale:** Create a ten-step value scale — ten squares in a row, progressing from white (the paper) at one end to the darkest black you can produce at the other, with eight evenly-spaced gray steps between. This exercise sounds simple. It is not. Producing even gradations requires control of pressure, media, and layering. Your value scale is your reference tool — keep it and use it to check your drawings.

**How Light Creates Form:** When light hits a three-dimensional object, it produces a predictable pattern:
- **Highlight** — the brightest spot where light hits most directly
- **Light side** — the area facing the light source, receiving direct illumination
- **Halftone** — the transitional zone where the surface turns away from the light
- **Core shadow** — the darkest area on the object itself, where the surface faces away from the light
- **Reflected light** — a subtle lightening within the shadow side, caused by light bouncing off nearby surfaces
- **Cast shadow** — the shadow thrown by the object onto an adjacent surface; darkest nearest the object and lighter as it extends

Learn this pattern. It applies to every object under every light source. Once you see it, you cannot unsee it — and your drawings will immediately improve because you will know where the darks and lights belong.

**Still Life with Value:** Set up a single white or light-colored sphere (or an egg, or a ball) under a single lamp. Draw it, rendering the full value pattern: highlight, light side, halftone, core shadow, reflected light, cast shadow. Then do the same with a cylinder (a can or bottle) and a cube (a box). These three forms — sphere, cylinder, cube — are the building blocks. Every complex form can be understood as combinations of these basic shapes.

### Part 2: Proportion and Measurement

Your eye is easily deceived about proportion. The brain has systematic biases: it makes the eyes too high on the head (because the face is more "important" than the cranium), it makes hands too small (because it minimizes what it considers secondary), and it compensates for perspective (drawing what it "knows" instead of what it sees). Measurement corrects these biases.

**Sight-Size Measurement:** Hold your pencil at full arm's length (arm straight, elbow locked). Close one eye. Align the top of your pencil with the top of the object you are measuring. Slide your thumb down to mark the bottom. Now, without moving your thumb, compare that measurement to another part of the subject. How many times does the apple's height fit into the bottle's height? Is the vase wider than it is tall, or taller than it is wide?

This is not a trick — it is the method observational artists have used for centuries. Practice it until it becomes automatic. Every time you draw, measure before you commit.

**The Head as a Unit:** For figure drawing, the human body has reliable proportional relationships: the average adult is approximately 7.5 heads tall. The eyes fall at the vertical midpoint of the head (not higher — this is the most common proportion error in portrait drawing). The hand is approximately the same size as the face from chin to hairline. The elbow falls at the waist. These relationships allow you to check your observations and catch errors.

**Common Errors to Watch For:**
- Eyes placed too high on the head — the cranium is larger than most people assume
- Hands and feet drawn too small — the brain minimizes extremities
- Near objects drawn too small — the brain compensates for perspective and resists drawing the dramatic size differences that perspective actually produces
- Symmetry imposed where none exists — real faces, bodies, and objects are asymmetrical

### Part 3: Perspective

Perspective is not a style. It is a description of how three-dimensional space actually appears on a two-dimensional plane. Understanding perspective does not limit your drawing — it frees it, because you can construct convincing space whenever you need it.

**One-Point Perspective:** When you look straight down a hallway, all the parallel lines — the edges of the floor, the ceiling, the tops and bottoms of the walls — converge toward a single point on the horizon. This is the vanishing point. To construct a one-point perspective drawing: establish a horizon line (your eye level), place a vanishing point on it, and draw all receding parallel lines toward that point. Verticals remain vertical. Horizontals remain horizontal. Only the lines going away from you converge.

Practice: Draw a room in one-point perspective. Draw a road with trees on either side. Draw a row of buildings receding into the distance.

**Two-Point Perspective:** When you see an object from a corner (a building seen at an angle, a box turned on the table), two sets of parallel lines recede toward two different vanishing points, both on the horizon line. The only truly vertical lines are the vertical edges nearest you. Everything else angles toward one vanishing point or the other.

Practice: Draw a building from its corner. Draw several boxes at different angles. Draw a street scene where buildings are visible at angles.

**Atmospheric Perspective:** As objects recede in distance, they appear lighter in value, lower in contrast, less detailed, and cooler in color. This is caused by the atmosphere between you and the distant object. In drawing, you can create powerful depth by simply making foreground elements darker and more detailed while keeping background elements lighter and simpler.

### Part 4: Figure Drawing

The human figure is the most complex and rewarding subject in drawing. It combines every skill: proportion, value, gesture, anatomy, and composition.

**Gesture Drawing:** Quick drawings (30 seconds to 2 minutes) that capture the essential movement and energy of a pose. Gesture drawing is not about accuracy — it is about the line of action, the weight, the dynamic flow of the body. Use the full arm, not just the wrist. Fill the page. A good gesture drawing looks alive even though it has almost no detail.

Practice gesture drawing regularly — at least weekly. Use live models when available (family members holding poses), or figure reference photographs. Do at least 10 gesture drawings per session.

**Basic Anatomy for Artists:** You do not need to memorize every muscle. You need to understand the major landmarks that determine the visible form: the ribcage (an egg-shaped mass), the pelvis (a bowl-shaped mass), the spine (connecting and articulating them), and the shoulder girdle. The major muscle groups that create surface form: the deltoid (shoulder), pectoralis (chest), latissimus dorsi (back), quadriceps (front of thigh), gastrocnemius (calf). Understanding what is beneath the skin allows you to draw the surface convincingly.

**Portrait Drawing:** The human face follows reliable proportional relationships: the head is roughly egg-shaped; the eyes sit at the vertical midpoint; the base of the nose is halfway between the eyes and the chin; the mouth is roughly one-third of the way from nose to chin; the ears extend from eye level to nose level. These are starting points for observation, not formulas — every face departs from the average, and the departures are what make a portrait look like the specific person.

### Part 5: Media and Practice

**Graphite** allows fine detail and precise value control. Use softer pencils (4B-8B) for dark values and harder pencils (HB-2H) for light values and fine lines. Build values through layering — multiple light passes produce richer darks than pressing hard.

**Charcoal** covers large areas quickly and erases easily. Vine charcoal is delicate and erasable; compressed charcoal is darker and more permanent. Charcoal is ideal for gesture drawing, value studies, and large-scale work. It requires fixative to prevent smudging.

**Sketchbook Practice:** Maintain a daily or near-daily sketchbook. Draw from life whenever possible: people in cafes, objects on your desk, the view from your window, your own reflection. The sketchbook is your training ground — it does not need to be pretty. It needs to be used.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Value Scale and Form Studies [Early Packet]
Create the ten-step value scale. Then produce three form studies: a sphere, a cylinder, and a cube under single-source lighting, each rendered with the full light-to-shadow pattern. These are foundational exercises — take them seriously.

### Study 2: Still Life Series with Value [Ongoing]
Set up and draw a new still life every 1-2 weeks. Progress from simple (3 objects, clear light) to complex (5+ objects, draped cloth, varied materials). Each drawing should demonstrate full value rendering and accurate proportion. Spend 1-3 hours per drawing.

### Study 3: Perspective Construction [Mid-Packet]
Complete a series of perspective exercises: one-point room interior, one-point street scene, two-point building exterior, two-point object study. First construct these with ruler and vanishing points. Then draw similar scenes from observation, using your understanding of perspective to guide what you see.

### Study 4: Gesture Drawing Sessions [Weekly]
Weekly sessions of at least 20 gesture drawings at 30 seconds to 2 minutes each. Use live models or figure reference. Focus on the line of action and the overall movement, not detail. Keep all gesture drawings — your improvement over weeks will be dramatic.

### Study 5: Portrait Study [Late Packet]
Draw at least three portraits from observation: a family member, a friend, a self-portrait in a mirror. Focus on proportional placement of features and value rendering of the facial planes. Compare your proportions to the standard landmarks (eyes at midpoint, etc.) and note where the specific face departs from the average.

### Study 6: Master Drawing Analysis [Ongoing]
Study one master drawing per month: analyze the composition, value structure, line quality, and technical approach. Then copy a portion of it — not to imitate, but to understand the decisions the artist made. Suggested artists: Rembrandt (selective detail and shadow), Durer (precision and surface), Degas (composition and movement), Kollwitz (expressive power), Michelangelo (anatomy and form).

### Study 7: Extended Observational Drawing [End of Packet]
Produce one extended drawing that requires multiple sessions (at least 3 hours total): a complex still life, a detailed landscape, or a sustained figure study. This drawing demonstrates the integration of all skills: value, proportion, perspective, and sustained attention.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Value Rendering
**Developing well:** Still life drawings show the full light-to-shadow progression. Forms look three-dimensional. The student uses a range of values from paper-white to near-black. They understand reflected light and core shadow as distinct phenomena.

**Developing slowly:** Drawings are flat — outlines filled in with uniform gray. The student has not connected the concept of value to the reality of light on form.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student produced the value scale? Have they drawn the basic forms (sphere, cylinder, cube) under controlled lighting? Often the breakthrough comes from doing these simple exercises properly before moving to complex still lifes.

### 2. Proportional Accuracy
**Developing well:** The student uses sight-size measurement habitually. Proportions in still life and figure work are increasingly accurate. The student can identify their own proportion errors before being told.

**Developing slowly:** Proportions are consistently off — heads too small, hands too tiny, features too high — and the student does not notice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student actually measuring, or just estimating? The method must be practiced physically — pencil at arm's length, one eye closed, thumb marking. Some students resist the method because it feels mechanical, but it works. Have the student measure, note the ratio, then check their drawing against it.

### 3. Perspective Understanding
**Developing well:** Constructed perspective drawings are accurate. Observational drawings show understanding of convergence and atmospheric perspective. The student can identify the horizon line and approximate vanishing points in any scene.

**Developing slowly:** Perspective exercises are confusing. The student cannot reliably construct a one-point room interior.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student spent time physically observing perspective — standing in a hallway, looking down a road? Sometimes the abstract construction only makes sense after the real-world observation is solid. Also: is the student using a ruler and working carefully, or trying to freehand the construction? The construction must be precise at first; freehand perspective comes later.

### 4. Figure Drawing
**Developing well:** Gesture drawings capture movement and weight. Longer figure studies show proportional understanding and value rendering on the form. The student is beginning to understand basic anatomy as it affects surface form.

**Developing slowly:** Gesture drawings are stiff and hesitant. The student draws details (eyes, fingers) before establishing the overall gesture. Figures are proportionally distorted in systematic ways.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student doing gesture drawings regularly? Gesture is a skill that develops through volume — dozens of drawings per session, many sessions over months. If the student is only drawing one or two figures per week, the practice is insufficient.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Jumping to Complex Subjects Too Soon
The student wants to draw portraits and full figures before mastering value on a sphere. Resist this. The basic form studies (sphere, cylinder, cube) are not boring warm-ups — they are the exercises that build the foundation. A student who can render a sphere convincingly can render anything. A student who skips to portraits without this foundation will struggle with every drawing.

### Allowing the Student to Avoid Measurement
Some students resist sight-size measurement because they want to draw "by feel." Feeling is important — but at Weathering Storms, it is not reliable yet. The student needs the measurement method as a check against the brain's systematic biases. Over time, their eye will become reliable and they will need to measure less. But now, they need the method.

### Over-Relying on Photographs
Drawing from photographs is convenient but inferior to drawing from life. Photographs flatten value, compress depth, and distort proportion (especially with wide-angle lenses). Use photographs as supplementary reference — especially for figure drawing when live models are unavailable — but the primary practice should be drawing from life.

### Neglecting the Sketchbook
The formal exercises (still life, perspective construction, gesture sessions) are essential but not sufficient. The sketchbook — daily, informal, observational — is where the habit of seeing is maintained and the volume of practice accumulates. A student who only draws during formal sessions draws too little.
